sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presents with right elbow pain that has been ongoing for three weeks.
He reports that the pain is worse with movement and better with rest.
He describes the pain as sharp, stabbing, and aching, and rates it an 8/10.
He has been playing tennis regularly and attributes the pain to overuse.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide significant relief.
He also reports trying ice, but it did not help.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma.
He has a known allergy to penicillin.
He had his gallbladder removed approximately 10 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any fever or other symptoms.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day, Flovent twice a day, Albuterol as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's cardiovascular exam is normal with a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, unchanged from prior exams.
The musculoskeletal exam reveals moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion.
There is no palpable joint effusion.
The patient experiences mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
Test Results: The right elbow X-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, with no malalignment or joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse.
He also has uncontrolled diabetes type two, and mild intermittent asthma that remains under good control.
Plan:
I recommend that the patient rest his right arm as much as possible, stop playing tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress on that arm.
I prescribe Ultram 50mg once a day for pain relief.
I also order a hemoglobin A1c level, CBC, and chem-12 blood test to monitor his diabetes.
I increase his metformin to 1000mg twice a day and instruct him to check his blood sugar twice daily for two weeks.
I write a prescription for a refill of Albuterol and instruct him to use it as needed.
I also provide a brace for his right forearm to help with discomfort.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in four weeks to reevaluate his elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
